writing_type: UX/product copy (error states, recovery flows, and transactional screens)
user_goals_summary: Writes interface copy that feels like a calm, competent presence alongside the user — not narrating their situation from above, not performing empathy, just plainly and precisely meeting them where they are. Every word must do specific work for the specific moment it appears in, and the user should always feel like the subject of the copy, never the object of it.

rubric.name: Tonal calibration
rubric.description: Copy hits a precise register: calm and grounded without being clinical, warm without being breezy, confident without cheerleading. The target is 'the copy equivalent of a calm nod' — the way a competent colleague would talk to you after a snag. No pep talks, no customer-service voice, no sardonic edge, no shrugging.

dimensions.label: Avoids pep-talk language, forced optimism, or performative warmth
Does not read like a system log, support article, or procedure document
Reads as grounded and steady rather than casual or dismissive
Never anticipates negative user behavior or introduces anxiety that wasn't already there
Sounds like something a real person would actually say — plain, direct, conversational without performing conversational

rubric.name: Contextual presence
rubric.description: Copy feels written for the specific screen, moment, and emotional state the user is in — not lifted from a generic pattern library. It should be clear from the copy alone what just happened, without the copy explicitly narrating or commenting on the situation. The line should feel like it knows where the user is.

dimensions.label: Copy would feel out of place on a different screen or in a different context
Addresses the question the user is silently asking, not an adjacent or assumed question
Acknowledges the situation without describing or commenting on it from the outside
Does not comment on the difficulty, commonality, or expected duration of the situation

rubric.name: Functional completeness
rubric.description: Every standalone piece of copy does its full job without depending on another element to close the loop. Messages name the problem, provide relevant context, and point to the next action — all within the same unit. A user who sees only that one element should have enough to act.

dimensions.label: Clearly states what happened or went wrong
Gives enough 'why' for the user to understand the situation without guessing
Includes a clear next step or direction within the same element
Element makes sense and feels complete if the user never sees the next screen

rubric.name: User-centered direction
rubric.description: The user is the subject of the copy, not the product or the situation. Copy puts the user in motion — they are the one acting, finding, fixing — rather than watching the product assess or narrate. CTAs tell the user exactly what will happen when they click. Second person, present tense by default.

dimensions.label: The user is positioned as the agent doing the action, not the product
Buttons and links describe the outcome of clicking, not vague verbs like 'try again' or 'submit'
Nothing in the copy implies the user made a mistake or did something wrong
Copy orients toward the next action rather than dwelling on or explaining the current state

rubric.name: Economy and precision
rubric.description: Every word does specific, identifiable work. No throat-clearing, no filler, no over-introducing self-explanatory elements. Copy is short and scannable. If a list has clear labels and descriptions, the line above it doesn't need to explain what the list is.

dimensions.label: No setup lines, preambles, or transition phrases that delay the useful content
Removing any word would change or lose the meaning
Structure supports quick visual parsing — short sentences, clear hierarchy, labeled sections
Does not introduce or describe elements that are already self-explanatory from their content

rubric.name: Trust through specificity
rubric.description: Reassurance is earned by naming concrete realities the user cares about — saved progress, nothing charged, specific causes — not by using reassurance language ('don't worry,' 'no problem'). Confidence comes from being specific, not from telling the user to feel confident. No hedging and no overclaiming.

dimensions.label: Reassuring statements reference specific, verifiable facts rather than emotional soothing
Avoids 'probably,' 'likely,' 'might,' or other language that undercuts the copy's own confidence
Does not promise outcomes the product cannot guarantee
Proactively resolves concerns the user would have but might not articulate (e.g., 'was I charged?' 'did I lose my cart?')

coaching_notes: This user has an extraordinarily fine-grained sense of tonal register — they can distinguish between 'calm' and 'breezy,' between 'confident' and 'cheerleading,' between 'present' and 'narrating.' Expect many rounds of iteration on tone, especially on transitional or introductory lines. They will consistently reject copy that feels generic or interchangeable across contexts, even if it's technically correct. Their north star is copy that feels like a competent person standing next to the user — not above the moment looking down at it. When they say 'present,' they mean the copy should feel like it was written by someone who knows what screen the user is on and what just happened, without explicitly saying so.

rubric.description: Every standalone piece of copy does its full job without depending on another element to close the loop. Messages name the problem, provide relevant context, and point to the next action — all within the same unit. A user who sees only that one element should have enough to act.

dimensions.label: Clearly states what happened or went wrong
Gives enough 'why' for the user to understand the situation without guessing
Includes a clear next step or direction within the same element
Element makes sense and feels complete if the user never sees the next screen

Every element must do its full job independently, CTAs must name concrete outcomes, and the voice should sound like a calm, competent person — not a product, not a support doc, not a pattern library. rubric.name: Functional completeness
rubric.description: Every standalone piece of copy does its full job without depending on another element to close the loop. Messages name the problem, provide relevant context, and point to the next action — all within the same unit. A user who sees only that one element should have enough to act.

dimensions.label: Clearly states what happened or what the current state is
Gives enough 'why' for the user to understand the situation without guessing
Includes a clear next step or direction within the same element
Element makes sense and feels complete if the user never sees the next screen
Headline, body, and CTA work as a single coherent unit — each does distinct work and together they form one piece of writing

rubric.name: User-centered direction
rubric.description: The user is the subject of the copy, not the product or the situation. Copy puts the user in motion toward a concrete outcome. CTAs name the literal thing the user gets after clicking — not the action, not a metaphor, not wordplay. Benefits are framed as what the user gains, not what they stop worrying about.

dimensions.label: The user is positioned as the agent acting, not the product assessing or narrating
Buttons name the concrete, literal result of clicking — not a vague verb, metaphor, or clever phrase
Benefits are framed as what the user gains or has afterward, not what they avoid or stop feeling
Nothing in the copy implies the user made a mistake or did something wrong
Copy orients toward what happens next rather than dwelling on or explaining the current state

rubric.name: Tonal calibration
rubric.description: Copy hits a precise register: calm and grounded without being clinical, warm without being breezy, confident without cheerleading. The target is the way a competent colleague would talk to you after a snag — plain, direct, present. The copy should contain at least one beat that sounds like a person actually said it, and the rhythm should vary so payoffs land like an exhale rather than another list item.

dimensions.label: Avoids pep-talk language, forced optimism, or performative warmth
Reads as steady and present — not clinical, not breezy, not sardonic, not like a support article
Contains at least one moment that sounds like something a real person would say out loud — offhand and plain, not performing conversational
Never introduces concerns the user wasn't already having, or anticipates negative behavior like abandoning
Register varies intentionally — not one flat temperature throughout; payoff moments land like an exhale, not a list item

rubric.name: Contextual presence
rubric.description: Copy feels written for the specific screen, moment, and emotional state the user is in — not lifted from a pattern library or generic troubleshooting template. It addresses the question the user is silently asking. All elements on the screen share the same voice and register, with no tonal gaps between headline, body, and CTA.

dimensions.label: Copy would feel out of place on a different screen or in a different context
Addresses the question the user is silently asking, not an adjacent or assumed question
Acknowledges the situation without describing, commenting on, or editorializing about it from the outside

rubric.name: Trust through specificity
rubric.description: Reassurance is earned by naming concrete realities the user cares about — saved progress, nothing charged, specific causes — not by using reassurance language. Confidence comes from being specific, not from telling the user to feel confident. No hedging and no overclaiming.

dimensions.label: Reassuring statements reference specific, verifiable facts rather than emotional soothing

Does not promise outcomes the product cannot guarantee or use metaphors that overstate capability
Proactively resolves concerns the user would have but might not articulate (e.g., 'was I charged?' 'did I lose my progress?')

rubric.name: Economy and precision
rubric.description: Every word does specific, identifiable work. No throat-clearing, no filler, no over-introducing self-explanatory elements. When one strong statement can close a thought, it is not expanded into a catalogue of benefits. Completeness never justifies length.

dimensions.label: No setup lines, preambles, or transition phrases that delay the useful content
Removing any word would change or lose the meaning
Structure supports quick visual parsing — short sentences, clear hierarchy, labeled sections where needed
Does not introduce or describe elements that are already self-explanatory from their content
When one strong closing statement can do the job, avoids expanding into a list of benefits or features

coaching_notes: This user iterates relentlessly on tonal calibration — they distinguish between 'calm and present' and 'calm and procedural,' and will not settle for the latter. Non-negotiables: every standalone element must do its full job; CTAs must name concrete outcomes (not metaphors, not clever wordplay); copy must never introduce anxiety or blame. When presenting options, lead with the one that does the least while saying the most — this user consistently picks the shortest, plainest option that still sounds human.

rubric.name: User-centered direction
rubric.description: The user is the subject of the copy, not the product or the situation. Copy puts the user in motion toward a concrete outcome. CTAs name the literal result the user gets after clicking — not the action they're performing, not a metaphor, not clever wordplay. The user should know what happens before they click. Benefits are framed as what the user gains, not what they stop worrying about.

dimensions.label: The user is positioned as the agent acting, not the product assessing or narrating

Button labels describe the outcome, not the action — 'Save changes' over 'Submit', 'Start free trial' over 'Sign up'. Outcome framing should be lightweight — 'Add Payee' satisfies this because a payee getting added is the outcome; you don't need to spell it out as 'Save Your First Payee'. CTA labels should remain accurate across repeat visits — avoid embedding session-specific framing like 'Your First' that breaks on the second use.
Benefits are framed as what the user gains or has afterward, not what they avoid or stop feeling
Nothing in the copy implies the user made a mistake or did something wrong
Copy orients toward what happens next rather than dwelling on or explaining the current state
Instructions use active voice — 'Enter your account number' not 'Your account number should be entered'

rubric.name: Functional completeness
rubric.description: Every standalone piece of copy does its full job without depending on another element to close the loop. Error messages follow the sequence: WHAT HAPPENED, WHY, WHAT TO DO — in that order. A vague error like 'Something went wrong' is a product failure, not just a copy failure. Confirmation dialogs name the specific consequence, not a generic prompt. Empty states guide the user toward their first action rather than announcing absence.

dimensions.label: Clearly states what happened or what the current state is
Gives enough 'why' for the user to understand the situation without guessing
Includes a clear next step or direction within the same element
Element makes sense and feels complete if the user never sees the next screen
Headline, body, and CTA work as a single coherent unit — each does distinct work and together they form one piece of writing
Error messages follow the what-why-what structure: what happened, why it happened, what to do next — no generic fallback copy
Confirmation dialogs name the specific consequence and scope — 'Delete 3 invoices permanently?' not 'Are you sure?'

Empty states direct the user toward their first action — they read as a helpful nudge, not a dead end. Empty-state copy guides toward action without coaching tone — the nudge is structural (a clear CTA), not verbal (motivational framing).

rubric.name: Tonal calibration
rubric.description: Tone shifts deliberately based on context. Celebratory moments (first successful payment, onboarding complete) can be warm. Error states should be calm and precise — never panicked, never dismissive. Routine actions should be nearly invisible. Applying a single flat tone across all contexts feels robotic and erodes trust. No marketing register inside the product itself.

dimensions.label: Avoids pep-talk language, forced optimism, or performative warmth in neutral or error contexts. Avoids pep-talk language in CTAs as well as body copy — a button label is not a place for encouragement or milestone-marking.
Error and warning states read as steady and precise — not panicked, not clinical, not sarcastic
Celebratory moments (first payment, milestone reached) are allowed to be warm — tone matches the emotional weight of the moment
Never introduces concerns the user wasn't already having, or anticipates negative behavior like abandoning
Register varies intentionally across screens — not one flat temperature throughout
Product UI copy does not use promotional or marketing register — no superlatives, no value-prop language inside the flow

rubric.name: Trust through specificity
rubric.description: Reassurance is earned by naming concrete realities the user cares about — saved progress, nothing charged, specific causes — not by using reassurance language. Confidence comes from being specific, not from telling the user to feel confident. No hedging and no overclaiming.

dimensions.label: Reassuring statements reference specific, verifiable facts rather than emotional soothing
Avoids 'probably,' 'likely,' 'might,' or other language that undercuts the copy's own confidence
Does not promise outcomes the product cannot guarantee or use metaphors that overstate capability
Proactively resolves concerns the user would have but might not articulate (e.g., 'was I charged?' 'did I lose my progress?')
Terminology is consistent across the entire flow — the same action, object, or state is never referred to by two different names

rubric.name: Contextual presence
rubric.description: Copy feels written for the specific screen, moment, and emotional state the user is in — not lifted from a pattern library or generic troubleshooting template. It addresses the question the user is silently asking. All elements on the screen share the same voice and register, with no tonal gaps between headline, body, and CTA.

dimensions.label: Copy would feel out of place on a different screen or in a different context
Addresses the question the user is silently asking, not an adjacent or assumed question

Acknowledges the situation without describing, commenting on, or editorializing about it from the outside. CTA labels do not narrate or editorialize — they don't tell the user what milestone they're reaching or reflect their journey back at them.
All elements on the screen share the same voice and register — no tonal gap between a system-label headline and a human-sounding body

Every element must do its full job independently, CTAs must name concrete outcomes, and the voice should sound like a calm, competent person — not a product, not a support doc, not a pattern library. The user values economy, but not at the cost of humanity.

Non-negotiables: every standalone element must do its full job; CTAs must name concrete outcomes (not metaphors, not clever wordplay); copy must never introduce anxiety or blame. When presenting options, lead with the one that does the least while saying the most — this user consistently picks the shortest, plainest option that still sounds human.

user_goals_summary: Writes product copy that feels calm, present, and written for the specific moment the user is in — not lifted from a pattern library. Values economy and precision but insists on at least one human beat per copy unit. Treats the user as a competent adult who needs clear direction, not coaching or cheerleading.

rubric.name: Economy and precision

dimensions.label: No setup lines, preambles, or transition phrases that delay the useful content
Removing any word would change or lose the meaning
Structure supports quick visual parsing — short sentences, clear hierarchy, labeled sections where needed
Does not introduce or describe elements that are already self-explanatory from their content
When one strong closing statement can do the job, avoids expanding into a list of benefits or features

rubric.name: Contextual presence
rubric.description: Copy feels written for the specific screen, moment, and emotional state the user is in — not lifted from a pattern library or generic troubleshooting template. It addresses the question the user is silently asking, not an adjacent one. All elements on the screen share the same voice, register, and assumed audience — no tonal gap between headline, body, and CTA.

dimensions.label: Copy would feel out of place on a different screen or in a different context — it clearly knows what just happened
Addresses the question the user is silently asking — not an adjacent question like how long it will take or how common it is
Acknowledges the situation without describing, commenting on, or editorializing about it from the outside — copy is in the moment, not above it
All elements on the screen share the same voice, register, and assumed audience — no tonal gap between a system-label headline and a human-sounding body
Contains at least one moment that sounds like something a real person would say out loud — offhand and plain, not performing conversational

rubric.name: Tonal calibration
rubric.description: Tone shifts deliberately based on context. Celebratory or first-time moments can carry warmth. Error states should be calm and precise — never panicked, never dismissive. Routine actions should be nearly invisible. No marketing register inside the product. The target is how a competent, slightly knowing colleague would talk to you — plain, direct, present. Never a pep talk, never a shrug.

dimensions.label: Avoids pep-talk language, forced optimism, or performative warmth — in body copy, headlines, and button labels alike
Error and warning states read as steady and precise — not panicked, not clinical, not sarcastic, not breezy
First-time moments and celebratory moments are allowed to be warm — tone matches the emotional weight of the moment
Never introduces concerns the user wasn't already having, or anticipates negative behavior like abandoning
Product UI copy does not use promotional or marketing register — no superlatives, no value-prop language inside the flow

rubric.name: User-centered direction
rubric.description: The user is the subject of the copy, not the product or the situation. Copy puts the user in motion toward a concrete outcome. CTAs make it clear what clicking achieves — simple, literal labels are preferred when the action IS the outcome. Benefits are framed as what the user gains, not what they stop worrying about.

dimensions.label: The user is positioned as the agent acting, not the product assessing, investigating, or narrating
Button labels make it clear what clicking achieves — 'Add Payee' is fine when a payee being added IS the outcome; 'Resubmit order' over 'Try again'; no metaphors or clever wordplay
CTA labels remain accurate across repeat visits — no session-specific framing like 'Your First' that breaks on the second use
Benefits are framed as what the user gains or has afterward, not what they avoid or stop feeling
Nothing in the copy implies the user made a mistake or did something wrong
Instructions use active voice — 'Enter your account number' not 'Your account number should be entered'

rubric.name: Functional completeness
rubric.description: Every standalone piece of copy does its full job without depending on another element to close the loop. Error messages follow the sequence: what happened, why, what to do — in that order. Confirmation dialogs name the specific consequence. Empty states guide the user toward their first action rather than announcing absence. A user who sees only one element should have enough to act.

dimensions.label: Error messages follow the what-why-what structure: what happened, why it happened, what to do next — no generic fallback copy
Each element makes sense and feels complete if the user never sees the next screen
Empty states direct the user toward their first action — they read as a helpful nudge, not a dead end or a system status label
Confirmation dialogs name the specific consequence and scope — 'Delete 3 invoices permanently?' not 'Are you sure?'
Headline, body, and CTA work as a single coherent unit — each does distinct work and together they form one escalating thought

rubric.name: Trust through specificity
rubric.description: Reassurance is earned by naming concrete realities the user cares about — saved progress, nothing charged, specific causes — not by using reassurance language. Confidence comes from being specific, not from telling the user to feel confident. No hedging and no overclaiming.

dimensions.label: Reassuring statements reference specific, verifiable facts rather than emotional soothing
Avoids 'probably,' 'likely,' 'might,' or other language that undercuts the copy's own confidence
Does not promise outcomes the product cannot guarantee or use metaphors that overstate capability
Proactively resolves concerns the user would have but might not articulate — 'was I charged?', 'did I lose my progress?'
Terminology is consistent across the entire flow — the same action, object, or state is never referred to by two different names

coaching_notes: Non-negotiable: every piece of copy must be functionally self-contained, and CTAs must be clear about what clicking does — no cleverness, no metaphors, no session-specific framing that breaks on repeat visits. Trade-off pattern: will sacrifice brevity for a 'human beat' (one offhand, knowing line per unit) but will not sacrifice clarity for personality. Responds best to options presented with short rationales; iterates by describing what's wrong with increasing precision until the exact register clicks — expect many rounds on tonal nuance, especially for opening lines in error and empty states.

rubric.name: User-centered direction
rubric.description: The user is the subject of the copy, not the product or the situation. Copy puts the user in motion toward a concrete outcome. CTAs describe what clicking achieves — which is sometimes an action the user came to take, not a state they'll arrive in afterward. Benefits are framed as what the user gains, not what they stop worrying about.

dimensions.label: The user is positioned as the agent acting, not the product assessing, investigating, or narrating

Button labels make clear what clicking achieves — prefer outcome framing unless the action itself is the outcome the user came to complete ('Start Sending Payments', 'Add Payee'); avoid labels like 'Set Up Payments' that imply more work remains or reframe the user's goal as configuration; no metaphors or clever wordplay
CTA labels remain accurate across repeat visits — no session-specific framing like 'Your First' that breaks on the second use
Benefits are framed as what the user gains or has afterward, not what they avoid or stop feeling
Nothing in the copy implies the user made a mistake or did something wrong
Instructions use active voice — 'Enter your account number' not 'Your account number should be entered'

rubric.name: Tonal calibration
rubric.description: Tone shifts deliberately based on context. Celebratory or first-time moments can carry warmth. Error states should be calm and precise — never panicked, never dismissive. Routine actions should be nearly invisible. No marketing register inside the product. Applying the same tone everywhere feels robotic — the copy should match the emotional weight of the moment.

dimensions.label: Avoids pep-talk language, forced optimism, or performative warmth — in body copy, headlines, and button labels alike
Error and warning states read as steady and precise — not panicked, not clinical, not sarcastic, not breezy
First-time moments and celebratory moments are allowed to be warm — tone matches the emotional weight of the moment
Never introduces concerns the user wasn't already having, or anticipates negative behavior like abandoning
Product UI copy does not use promotional or marketing register — no superlatives, no value-prop language inside the flow
Routine, repetitive actions use copy that is as invisible as possible — no warmth or personality injected into mundane confirmations

rubric.name: Terminology consistency
rubric.description: The same thing is always called the same thing, everywhere in the flow. If the UI calls it a 'payment', no screen in the same flow calls it a 'transaction' or a 'transfer'. Inconsistent terminology creates silent doubt — the user wonders if these are different things. No placeholder text ships to production.

dimensions.label: The same entity, action, or state uses the same label throughout the entire flow — no synonyms introduced mid-journey
No placeholder text ('Lorem ipsum', 'TBD', 'Label here') appears in any production-facing element
Terminology introduced in early steps is carried forward exactly — if step 1 says 'payee', step 3 does not say 'recipient'

rubric.name: Economy and precision
rubric.description: Every word does specific, identifiable work. No throat-clearing, no filler, no over-introducing self-explanatory elements. When one strong statement can close a thought, it is not expanded into a catalogue of benefits. Completeness never justifies length.

dimensions.label: No setup lines, preambles, or transition phrases that delay the useful content
Removing any word would change or lose the meaning
Structure supports quick visual parsing — short sentences, clear hierarchy, labeled sections where needed

Values economy and precision but insists on at least one human beat per copy unit. Treats the user as a competent adult who needs clear direction, not coaching or cheerleading.
coaching_notes: Non-negotiable: every piece of copy must be functionally self-contained, and CTAs must be clear about what clicking does — no cleverness, no metaphors, no session-specific framing that breaks on repeat visits. user_goals_summary: Writes UI copy for payment product flows — error states, empty states, confirmations — targeting busy, competent users (often small business owners). Values copy that is calm, precise, and contextually present: each element does its full job, knows what moment it's in, and assumes the user's real-world competence. Tonal precision is paramount — different elements on the same screen should do distinct emotional work, like the same voice shifting gears.

rubric.name: Functional completeness
rubric.description: Every standalone piece of copy does its full job without depending on another element to close the loop. Error messages follow the sequence: what happened, why, what to do — in that order. Confirmation dialogs name the specific consequence. Empty states guide the user toward their first action rather than announcing absence. A user who sees only one element should have enough to act.

dimensions.label: Error messages follow the what-why-what structure: what happened, why it happened, what to do next — no generic fallback copy like 'Something went wrong'
Each element makes sense and feels complete if the user never sees the next screen
Empty states direct the user toward their first action — they read as a helpful nudge, not a dead end or a system status label
Confirmation dialogs name the specific consequence and scope — 'Delete 3 invoices permanently?' not 'Are you sure?'
Headline, body, and CTA work as a single coherent unit — each does distinct work and together they form one escalating thought, not three separate ideas on the same screen

rubric.name: Tonal calibration
rubric.description: Tone shifts deliberately based on context and position within a screen. First-time moments can carry warmth; error states should be calm and precise; routine actions should be nearly invisible. Elements on the same screen should do distinct emotional jobs — like the same voice shifting gears, not one flat temperature throughout. No marketing register inside the product.

dimensions.label: Avoids pep-talk language, forced optimism, performative warmth, shrugs, and hedging — no 'you've got this,' no 'no worries,' no 'likely' covering the copy's confidence
Error and warning states read as steady and precise — not panicked, not clinical, not sarcastic, not breezy
Elements within a single screen do distinct emotional jobs — e.g., headline holds the warmth, body shifts to practical, CTA commits to action — same voice, different gears
Product UI copy does not use promotional register, superlatives, or value-prop language inside the flow
Routine, repetitive actions use copy that is as invisible as possible — no warmth or personality injected into mundane confirmations

rubric.name: Contextual presence
rubric.description: Copy feels written for the specific screen, moment, and emotional state the user is in — not lifted from a pattern library or generic troubleshooting template. It addresses the question the user is silently asking, not an adjacent one. It assumes the user's real-world competence and context rather than explaining their own domain to them.

dimensions.label: Copy would feel out of place on a different screen or in a different context — it clearly knows what just happened and where the user is
Addresses the question the user is silently asking — not an adjacent question like how long it will take, how common it is, or how easy it is
Acknowledges the situation without describing, commenting on, or editorializing about it from the outside — copy is in the moment, not above it looking down
Treats the user as someone with existing knowledge and real-world context — a business owner who knows their vendors, not a persona being onboarded through a concept
Never introduces concerns the user wasn't already having — no preemptive framing around abandonment, difficulty, or failure

rubric.name: User-centered direction
rubric.description: The user is the subject of the copy, not the product or the situation. Copy puts the user in motion toward a concrete outcome. CTAs tell the user what happens when they click — defined from the user's perspective, not the system's. Sometimes the action itself IS the outcome ('Add a Payee'); sometimes the outcome is a capability being activated ('Start Sending Payments'). Never reframe the user's goal as configuration ('Set Up Payments').

dimensions.label: The user is positioned as the agent acting, not the product assessing, investigating, or narrating about the situation

Button labels tell the user what clicking achieves — the action itself when that IS the goal ('Add a Payee'), or the capability being unlocked ('Start Sending Payments'); never vague ('Try again'), never metaphorical ('Get Them Out of Your Head'), never system-framed ('Set Up Payments')
CTA labels remain accurate across repeat visits — no session-specific framing like 'Your First' that breaks on the second use
Benefits are framed as what the user gains or has afterward, not what they avoid or stop feeling — no away-from framing
Nothing in the copy implies the user made a mistake or did something wrong

rubric.name: Economy and precision
rubric.description: Every word does specific, identifiable work. No throat-clearing, no filler, no over-introducing self-explanatory elements. When one strong statement can close a thought, it is not expanded into a catalogue of benefits. Payoffs land like an exhale, not a list item.

dimensions.label: No setup lines, preambles, or transition phrases that delay the useful content
Removing any word would change or lose the meaning
Structure supports quick visual parsing — short sentences, clear hierarchy, labeled sections where needed
When one strong closing statement can do the job, avoids expanding into a list of benefits or features — 'After that, it just runs' over 'no due dates, no scrambles, nothing to remember'
Does not introduce or describe elements that are already self-explanatory from their content — no lines that exist only to set up what follows

rubric.name: Terminology consistency
rubric.description: The same thing is always called the same thing, everywhere in the flow. If the UI calls it a 'payment', no screen in the same flow calls it a 'transaction' or a 'transfer'. Inconsistent terminology creates silent doubt — the user wonders if these are different things.

dimensions.label: The same entity, action, or state uses the same label throughout the entire flow — no synonyms introduced mid-journey
No placeholder text ('Lorem ipsum', 'TBD', 'Label here') appears in any production-facing element
Terminology introduced in early steps is carried forward exactly — if step 1 says 'payee', step 3 does not say 'recipient'

coaching_notes: This user has extraordinarily precise tonal sensibility — they can feel the difference between 'plain' and 'clinical,' between 'warm' and 'cheerleading,' between 'present' and 'narrating.' They will often approve a line in isolation and then revisit it once they see it in context with the full screen unit; early approval is not final. When they say a CTA should 'name the outcome,' they mean the outcome as the USER defines it — which is sometimes the high-level action itself ('Start Sending Payments'), not a system-reframing of it ('Set Up Payments'). Their non-negotiable is that copy never feels lifted from a pattern library — every line must feel written for the specific person in the specific moment, without commentary, narration, or editorializing from the outside.

Empty states guide the user toward their first action rather than announcing absence. A user who sees only one element should have enough to act.
